text: I can handle that.
Can you send me the report?
Could you repeat that, please?
Can I ask you something?

I like working with brokers.
I want to grow in this company.
I'd like to learn the underwriting side.
Sofia likes meeting clients.
Paula, thanks for the time. Thirty minutes, one question, and then I let you go. What do you want?
Nobody asks me that. Can I think for a second?
Take your time. Let's start with the easy one: what do you like about your work?
I like working with brokers. Twenty years, and I still like it.
Good. Now the other one. In twelve months — what do you want?
I want to grow in this company. I want a challenge — I'd like to work with the international clients, not only the Brazilian ones.
That's the first time today somebody said it in one sentence. And what would you like to learn?
I'd like to improve my English. This conversation is in English, and I am still here.
Paula, your English is not the problem. Your English got you this meeting. Send me two lines: your goal, and what you'd like to learn. That's all I need.
One more thing. This is a great opportunity for me, and I want it.
I know. You just said it out loud. That's the whole meeting, Paula.
I'd like to improve my English.
My goal is to work with international clients.
This is a great opportunity for me.

Hi, Paula. David here. Your career conversation with Greg is on Thursday, and I want to tell you one thing about it, because nobody tells you this. Greg is going to ask you one question: What do you want? And I know exactly what you are going to do, because everybody does it. You are going to say: I'm happy, everything is fine, thank you. And then you are going to go back to your desk and be angry for a year. Here is the thing about English that helps you here. In your language, the word for want sounds like a child asking for chocolate — so you learned to hide it. In English, I want to grow is not rude. It is not arrogant. It is the answer to the question. It is what he asked for. And if you want it softer, you have the same trick you learned last week with can and could: I would like to grow. Same want, with a tie on. Both are correct, Paula. What is not correct is saying nothing. Two more things, and they are small. One: like is about what you already do — I like working with brokers. Say that one first, it is the easy one. Two: after want, the verb needs the to. I want to grow, never I want grow. Last week I told you the verb goes naked after can. This week it wears the to. I know. English does that. See you Thursday.

Good morning, Paula. Sofia here. Thursday, with Greg — one thing, and then I stop. I have watched you for three months. You are the best person in this office with a broker on the phone, and everybody here knows it. Everybody except you. Now, Thursday. He is going to ask what you want, and you are going to want to be modest. Don't. I did that for twelve years. Twelve years of saying I'm fine, everything is good, thank you — and every year somebody else got the opportunity, and I went home and told my husband it was not fair. It was fair, Paula. They asked me what I wanted, and I said nothing. Here is what I want you to understand, and it has nothing to do with English. You think saying what you want is arrogant. It is not. It is information. Greg cannot give you a challenge he does not know you want. He is not a magician — he is a director with a form. Fill in the form. And one last thing, because I know you: your English is not going to be perfect on Thursday, and Greg is not going to care. He is going to hear I want to grow, and he is going to write it down. That is all that happens. See you Thursday.
